{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Type Hints & Static Analysis
  1.3  Project Structure
  1.4  Virtual Environments & Dependency Management
  1.5  Documentation
  1.6  Error Handling
  1.7  Performance & Idiomatic Python
  1.8  Security Best Practices

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Most Popular Third-Party)
  2.4  nose2
  2.5  doctest
  2.6  Framework Comparison Table
  2.7  Testing Best Practices

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (no tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Use blank lines to separate top-level functions and class definitions.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Third-party imports
    3. Local application imports
- Use snake_case for functions and variables, PascalCase for classes,
  UPPER_CASE for constants.
- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Use trailing commas in multi-line constructs for cleaner diffs.

Tools: black (auto-formatter), flake8 (linter), isort (import sorting),
       ruff (fast all-in-one linter/formatter).

1.2 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
- Use type hints for function signatures and complex variables (PEP 484).
- Use mypy, pyright, or pyre for static type checking.
- Use Optional[X] instead of X | None for Python < 3.10.
- Use Protocol classes for structural subtyping (duck typing with types).
- Use TypedDict for dictionaries with known key schemas.
- Use Final to indicate constants that should not be reassigned.

Example:
    from typing import Optional

    def greet(name: str, age: Optional[int] = None) -> str:
        if age:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}!"

1.3 PROJECT STRUCTURE
----------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project metadata & tool config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

- Use the src-layout (src/ directory) to prevent accidental imports.
- Keep tests/ parallel to src/ at the project root.
- Use pyproject.toml for metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration.

1.4 VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS & DEPENDENCY MANAGEMENT
-------------------------------------------------
- Always use a virtual environment per project (venv, virtualenv, or conda).
- Pin dependencies with requirements.txt (simple) or use modern tools:
    - pipenv: Pipfile + Pipfile.lock
    - poetry: pyproject.toml + poetry.lock (recommended for libraries)
    - pip-tools: requirements.in + requirements.txt (compiled)
    - uv: Fast, modern Python package installer and resolver (by Astral)
- Separate dev dependencies (testing, linting) from production dependencies.
- Use pip freeze > requirements.txt only for reproducible lock files.

1.5 DOCUMENTATION
------------------
- Use docstrings for all public modules, classes, and functions (PEP 257).
- Prefer Google-style or NumPy-style docstrings for readability.
- Use Sphinx for generating HTML/PDF documentation.
- Maintain a

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------

    2. Third-party imports
    3. Local application imports
- Use snake_case for functions and variables, PascalCase for classes,

- Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines.
- Use trailing commas in multi-line constructs for cleaner diffs.

Tools: black (auto-formatter), flake8 (linter), isort (import sorting),
       ruff (fast all-in-one linter/formatter).

1.2 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
---------------------------------
- Use type hints for function signatures and complex variables (PEP 484).
- Use mypy, pyright, or pyre for static type checking.

- Use Protocol classes for structural subtyping (duck typing with types).
- Use TypedDict for dictionaries with known key schemas.
- Use Final to indicate constants that should not be reassigned.

Example:

        if age:
            return f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old."
        return f"Hello {name}!"

1.3 PROJECT STRUCTURE
----------------------
A well-organized Python project typically follows this layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project metadata & tool config
    ├── LICENSE
    ├── .gitignore
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module_a.py
    │       └── module_b.py
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module_a.py
    │   └── test_module_b.py
    ├── docs/
    └── scripts/

- Use the src-layout (src/ directory) to prevent accidental imports.
- Keep tests/ parallel to src/ at the project root.
- Use pyproject.toml for metadata, dependencies, and tool configuration.

1.4 VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS & DEPENDENCY MANAGEMENT
-------------------------------------------------
- Always use a virtual environment per project (venv, virtualenv, or conda).
- Pin dependencies with requirements.txt (simple) or use modern tools:
    - pipenv: Pipfile + Pipfile.lock
    - poetry: pyproject.toml + poetry.lock (recommended for libraries)
    - pip-tools: requirements.in + requirements.txt (compiled)
    - uv: Fast, modern Python package installer and resolver (by Astral)
- Separate dev dependencies (testing, linting) from production dependencies.
- Use pip freeze > requirements.txt only for reproducible lock files.

1.5 DOCUMENTATION
------------------
- Use docstrings for all public modules, classes, and functions (PEP 257).
- Prefer Google-style or NumPy-style docstrings for readability.
- Use Sphinx for generating HTML/PDF documentation.
- Maintain a README.md with installation, usage, and contribution info.
- Document complex logic with inline comments (explain "why", not "what").

Google-style docstring example:
    def fetch_user(user
